A minimal, distraction-free focus timer. Classic 25/5 pomodoro or flowmodoro — focus as long as you flow, break for a fifth of it.

## About this tool

Two timers in one, and nothing else on the screen:

- **Pomodoro** — the classic: 25 minutes of focus, 5 minutes of break, on repeat.
- **Flowmodoro** — no fixed blocks. Focus as long as you're in flow; when you take a break, you earn one fifth of your focus time.

It also keeps simple statistics for the day — total focus time, sessions,
average focus length, and your focus : break ratio — stored only in your
browser.

- Runs entirely in your browser — nothing is sent anywhere
- Works offline once loaded
- Keyboard friendly: <kbd>Space</kbd> to start / take a break, <kbd>S</kbd> to stop

If it helps you, share it with a friend.

A short, practical ebook: 27 checkpoints I run through before launching anything — from side project to production app.

## What's inside

- The **pre-launch checklist** — 27 checkpoints covering copy, performance,
  SEO basics, and the stuff everyone forgets (favicons, OG images, 404 pages).
- **Launch-day playbook** — where to post, in what order, and why timing
  matters less than you think.
- **Post-launch triage** — how to read your first week of feedback without
  overreacting to it.

## Who it's for

Anyone who builds and ships on the web — indie hackers, freelancers, or
developers launching their first side project.

What’s inside

  • The pre-launch checklist — 27 checkpoints covering copy, performance, SEO basics, and the stuff everyone forgets (favicons, OG images, 404 pages).
  • Launch-day playbook — where to post, in what order, and why timing matters less than you think.
  • Post-launch triage — how to read your first week of feedback without overreacting to it.

Where science meets sunnah — a daily rhythm planner combining ultradian focus cycles, circadian energy, and sunnah time management. Your day starts at Maghrib.

## About this tool

A daily rhythm planner built on a simple idea: the productivity science of
ultradian cycles and circadian energy fits naturally with how the sunnah
already structures a day.

- **Day starts at Maghrib** — the Islamic day begins at sunset, and so does your timeline (with both Gregorian and Hijri dates).
- **Ultradian cycles** — free time between anchors is filled with 90-minute deep-focus blocks and 20-minute breaks.
- **Circadian awareness** — each block is labeled with your likely energy level (peak, trough, rebound) so you match the task to the hour.
- **Sunnah anchors** — the five prayers, dhuha, qailulah before Dhuhr, and qiyamullail frame the schedule; a guide panel explains each.
- **Prayer times by location** — one tap fetches accurate times for where you are (with manual override).
- Write a target for every focus block, close the day with a reflection, and export it all to your clipboard.

Settings, notes, and reflections are saved in your browser — nothing is sent anywhere. Available in Bahasa Indonesia and English.

The tools I actually use every day
My honest daily stack — not the aspirational one, the real one.

Everyone posts their "stack". Here's mine, but honest — only things I open
every single day.

## Building

- **VS Code + Claude Code** — most of my code starts as a conversation now.
- **Astro** — for anything content-heavy, including this site.
- **TypeScript** — non-negotiable at this point.

## Thinking

- **Plain markdown files** — my notes, drafts, and plans all live in one
  folder of `.md` files. Searchable, portable, future-proof.

## The lesson

The pattern across all of these: **plain text wins**. Every tool I've kept
for more than a year stores its data in a format I could read with `cat`.

The lesson

The pattern across all of these: plain text wins. Every tool I’ve kept for more than a year stores its data in a format I could read with cat.
The single habit that changed how I build: cutting scope until shipping feels almost too easy.

The projects I've finished all have one thing in common: they were
embarrassingly small when they launched.

The ones I abandoned? All ambitious.

## Scope is the enemy

A feature you can ship this week beats a system you can ship this quarter.
Every extra week of building is a week of zero feedback — and feedback is
the only thing that tells you whether you're building the right thing at all.

> If you're not slightly embarrassed by v1, you shipped too late.

## My rule of thumb

Before starting anything, I ask: **what's the version of this I could ship
in two days?** Then I build *that*. Not as a prototype — as the actual
release.

Most of the time, the two-day version turns out to be all anyone needed.

The projects I’ve finished all have one thing in common: they were embarrassingly small when they launched.

The ones I abandoned? All ambitious.

Hello, world — why I built this site
The thinking behind wahyuteguh.com: a markdown-powered blog, a resources hub, and a home base I fully own.

Every builder needs a home base they fully own. Not a profile on someone
else's platform — an actual domain, with actual files, that will still work
in ten years.

This is mine.

## How it works

The whole site is static. Every blog post is just a markdown file in a
folder — I write, commit, and it's published. No CMS, no database, nothing
to maintain.

```
src/content/blog/
├── hello-world.md
├── shipping-small.md
└── ...
```

## What you'll find here

- **Blog** — long-form notes on software, product, and building independently.
- **Resources** — free tools that run in your browser, plus ebooks and
  templates you can download.

That's it. Simple on purpose.
